sector: Technology — Photonics / Optical Interconnect (AI scale-out + scale-up)
bull_thesis: Coherent is the broadest vertically-integrated photonics platform sitting in the AI optical-interconnect bottleneck — the physical layer that every dollar of hyperscaler and sovereign-AI capital spending has to pass through. The business has been transformed. Net leverage (net debt as a multiple of annual cash earnings) has been cut to 0.5x, down from 2.1x a year ago. Adjusted (non-GAAP) gross margin has expanded in 7 of the last 8 quarters, a cumulative gain of about 5.3 percentage points (530 basis points) to 39.6%, with a target above 42% from the company's Investor Day still to be delivered. Datacenter & Communications is now 75% of revenue and growing about 40% year over year (data center up about 37%, communications/scale-across up about 60%). In the March-quarter (FQ3 FY26), revenue was $1.806B (up about 21% on a reported basis and about 27% on a comparable, pro-forma basis year over year, and up about 9% from the prior quarter), adjusted earnings per share were $1.41 (up about 55% year over year), and operating margin was 20.3%. The next-quarter (FQ4) guide of $1.91–2.05B in revenue and $1.52–1.72 in EPS implies accelerating growth, and management explicitly guides for faster growth in fiscal 2027 than in fiscal 2026. The company is supply-constrained, not demand-constrained — record backlog, orders booked into calendar 2028, and long-term agreements running to the end of the decade that include customer upfront capital and minimum-demand commitments. The master variable is the 6-inch indium-phosphide ramp (the larger wafer size for the semiconductor material that makes the lasers, at the Sherman, Texas; Sweden; and Zurich sites): more than 4x the devices per wafer at under half the cost, doubling internal indium-phosphide capacity by the end of calendar 2026 (a quarter early) and quadrupling it by the end of calendar 2027 — at once the volume unlock and the gross-margin lever. On top of the core 800G/1.6T transceiver ramp (the higher-speed optical modules; 1.6T is ramping faster than expected, with a higher selling price than 800G), four stacked growth vectors layer in: optical circuit switching (a market estimated above $4B, doubled at the OFC industry conference, with the bottleneck just cleared); co-packaged optics, where the optics sit on the same package as the chip (an incremental market estimated above $15B, anchored by the March 2026 NVIDIA partnership — a $2B equity investment plus a multi-year supply agreement through the end of the decade, with scale-out revenue in the second half of calendar 2026 and scale-up in the second half of calendar 2027); multi-rail data-center interconnect (a market above $2B, first half of calendar 2027); and AI thermal solutions (Thermadite/thermoelectric, second half of calendar 2027). Pricing power is a quiet tailwind — 'pricing optimization' shows up repeatedly as a margin driver in a supply-short market. NVIDIA's $2B stake validates the platform and helps fund the capital spending. New operator-style management (CEO Jim Anderson, formerly of Lattice; CFO Sherri Luther) has been under-promising and over-delivering. The case holds if these conditions hold: AI capital spending stays durable; the indium-phosphide ramp stays on schedule; co-packaged-optics and optical-circuit-switching revenue arrive on the second-half-2026/2027 timeline; and gross margin keeps climbing toward the above-42% target.
bear_thesis: The business is excellent; the price is the entire problem. At about $389 per share, roughly $76B market value, about 52x forward earnings and about 9–10x enterprise value to sales (the company's value relative to its revenue), the stock is up about 171% over 12 months (from a $77.84 low to a $440 high) and sits near all-time highs — pricing in a vast amount of optionality from co-packaged optics, optical circuit switching, multi-rail and thermal that generates essentially zero revenue today (co-packaged optics starts in the second half of calendar 2026; multi-rail and thermal in calendar 2027). Free cash flow is deeply negative — March-quarter free cash flow was −$383M, trailing-twelve-month free cash flow is negative, and capital spending of $290M is rising — so the shares are valued as a cash machine the company is not yet. The above-42% gross-margin target is unmet (39.6%), and the sharpest analyst line (Barclays/Jefferies) — 'why isn't there more incremental margin fall-through given 6-inch indium phosphide at half the cost?' — was effectively conceded: most of the margin upside is ahead, not yet banked, meaning it depends on execution. Returns are unimpressive today (return on invested capital around 4%, return on equity around 5% over the trailing twelve months), held down by the heavy growth-spending phase and a large acquired-intangible base (about 43% of assets). Management deflected the head-to-head comparison with Lumentum on optical circuit switching and co-packaged optics — Coherent's actual share of these next-generation layers is unproven. The whole thesis rides on a single big-picture factor — the durability of AI capital spending — which is the same wall under every other name in the scale-out basket, so it adds no independent way for the bet to resolve and it sells off alongside the cohort on any hyperscaler capex scare. Technology-transition risk is live (copper hanging on in scale-up, rival co-packaged-optics designs, and the fork in the roadmap between silicon photonics, electro-absorption modulated lasers, and vertical-cavity lasers). The bear case is confirmed by any of: a slip in the indium-phosphide ramp (which would hit growth and margin at the same time), a push in the co-packaged-optics or optical-circuit-switching revenue timeline, or a single AI-capex guide-down from a hyperscaler. This is a great company at a demanding price inside a concentrated basket — and the catalyst (NVIDIA/co-packaged optics) has already largely fired into the +171% move.
sop_bull: $470–500 per share. This uses a whole-company, forward-earnings approach to the AI-optical franchise (valuing each business line separately is not warranted here — Datacenter & Communications is 75% of revenue and the value driver, while Industrial is a small, declining tail). The drivers: the midpoint of the FQ4 FY26 adjusted EPS guide, $1.62, annualizes to roughly a $6.5 exit run-rate; with fiscal 2027 growth faster than 2026 (accelerating revenue, gross margin marching toward above 42%, and operating leverage), fiscal 2027 adjusted EPS reaches about $9.0–9.5. A bull multiple of about 50x on $9.25 is about $460, plus the co-packaged-optics/optical-circuit-switching/multi-rail/thermal optionality (NVIDIA-anchored, with a co-packaged-optics market above $15B) carried as roughly a $10–40 option layer, getting to about $470–500. Cross-check: about 10x enterprise value to sales on a roughly $8–8.5B fiscal 2027 revenue run-rate is about $80–85B enterprise value, which is about $76B-plus of equity, or about $415–435 per share, with the optionality premium taking it to about $480. Net cash is modest (net debt of $1.83B), so there is no balance-sheet kicker; the $2B of NVIDIA cash is already in the cash line and funds capital spending.
sop_bear: $230–270 per share. This de-rates the stock to a market-respecting growth multiple as free cash flow stays negative and the priced-in optionality is discounted. Holding fiscal 2027 adjusted EPS to about $7.5 (a slower co-packaged-optics/optical-circuit-switching ramp, gross margin stalling about 40%, short of target) at about a 32–35x multiple gives about $240–265. Cross-check: about 5–6x enterprise value to sales on about $7.5B of forward revenue is about $40–45B enterprise value, or about $200–230 per share, plus a partial credit for co-packaged optics to about $255. This is where the stock trades if the durability of AI capital spending is questioned, the indium-phosphide ramp slips, or the cohort de-rates — roughly a 35–40% drawdown from the current price, back toward the 200-day average (about $224) plus the franchise premium.
sop_breakdown: Whole-company intrinsic value on the AI-optical franchise. Base case: fiscal 2027 adjusted (non-GAAP) earnings per share of about $8.5 (accelerating revenue, gross margin grinding from 39.6% toward — but not fully to — the above-42% target, plus operating leverage) at about a 40x multiple (a premium to the optical/AI-infrastructure cohort for the platform's breadth, the NVIDIA anchor, and a demonstrated margin trajectory, but a discount to the bull's 50x to reflect negative free cash flow and the dependence on a single big-picture macro factor) gives about $340. Add a modest $5–15 per share for the co-packaged-optics/optical-circuit-switching/multi-rail/thermal optionality, net of execution risk (NVIDIA-anchored but pre-revenue), getting to about $345–355. Enterprise-value-to-sales cross-check: about 8x on a roughly $8.3B fiscal 2027 revenue run-rate is about $66B enterprise value, or about $345 per share of equity. Net debt of $1.83B is immaterial to the per-share math. The Industrial segment (about 25% of revenue, declining) contributes under $30 per share at a mid-teens multiple and is a drag, not a sum-of-the-parts (valuing each business line separately) unlock. The base anchor is about $345 — roughly in line with the current price (about $389), or a modest downside of about 11%. The franchise quality is real, but the stock has already paid forward most of calendar 2026–27.
risk_reward: Roughly symmetric-to-unfavorable from the current price. The stock is about $389. Upside to the bull case of about $485 is +25%. Downside to the bear case of about $250 is −36%. Downside to the base case of about $345 is −11%. That is a risk/reward of about 0.7 to 1 against you (about 25% up versus about 36% down to the bear case). The asymmetry has compressed because the +171% move and the NVIDIA/co-packaged-optics catalyst have already largely fired — you are no longer being paid asymmetrically to own the optionality.
catalyst_status: FIRED (primary) + SOFT_EXTENDED (secondary). The load-bearing catalyst — the NVIDIA strategic partnership ($2B equity investment + multi-year CPO supply agreement through end of decade) — was ANNOUNCED March 2, 2026 and is the proximate driver of the +171%/12-month, near-all-time-high move; the re-rate is largely spent (FIRED). The forward catalysts (CPO scale-out revenue H2-CY26, scale-up H2-CY27, OCS ramp, multi-rail H1-CY27, thermal H2-CY27, InP doubling by end-CY26) are real, dated, and management-committed but are CAPACITY-RAMP / future-revenue milestones gated on the single shared AI-capex factor and on the company's own execution — SOFT_EXTENDED in the sense that they are serially forward-dated and not yet revenue-proven (CPO/OCS share vs Lumentum unquantified, mgmt deflected the comparison). Hard-dated near-term forcing function = the FQ4-FY26 print (~early Aug 2026) which must confirm the accelerating-growth + GM-toward-42% trajectory. Verified live June 2026.
peer_comps_note: True scale-out optical peers (live multiples verified June 2026): Lumentum (LITE) at about $850, roughly $66B market value — the most direct competitor on optical circuit switching and co-packaged optics, and the 'perceived gap' management was asked about; Lumentum screens at a far richer about 27x enterprise value to trailing sales (its depressed trailing sales are recovering), reflecting an even more aggressive AI-optical re-rate. Fabrinet (FN) at about $574, roughly $20.6B — the contract-manufacturing optical assembler (with NVIDIA/datacom transceiver exposure), a lower-multiple, lower-margin name at the 'value' end of the cohort. Credo (CRDO) at about $272, roughly $50.7B — the active-electrical-cables/serializer-deserializer plus silicon-photonics play, the hyper-growth/hyper-multiple name (stock near its 52-week high). Adjacent: Marvell (MRVL) at about $311 (custom silicon plus optical signal processing) and Nvidia (NVDA) at about $211 (the customer and sponsor). Where Coherent ranks: on valuation it is mid-cohort — about 9–10x enterprise value to sales and about 52x forward earnings is rich in absolute terms but cheaper than Lumentum (about 27x enterprise value to sales) and Credo, and richer than Fabrinet. On growth, Coherent's about 27% comparable year-over-year is solid but below Credo's hyper-growth, and accelerating. On margins, Coherent's 39.6% adjusted gross margin and 20.3% operating margin is mid-pack — better than Fabrinet (an assembler), below Credo's and Lumentum's peak-cycle. On returns, Coherent's return on invested capital of about 4% and return on equity of about 5% is the weakest in the cohort (a growth-spending and intangible drag) — a genuine red flag versus Lumentum's about 31% return on equity. On AI-capex exposure, Coherent has the broadest platform exposure (transceivers plus optical circuit switching plus co-packaged optics plus data-center interconnect plus thermal) and the marquee NVIDIA $2B anchor — arguably the highest-quality scale-out optionality, which is precisely why it is not cheap.
role_in_scaleout: Coherent is the broadest vertically-integrated photonics-materials supplier in the AI optical-interconnect bottleneck — it makes the indium-phosphide lasers (electro-absorption modulated and continuous-wave), silicon photonics, vertical-cavity lasers, fiber, lens arrays, isolators, and thermoelectric coolers that go into 800G/1.6T transceivers, optical-circuit-switching switches, data-center-interconnect/multi-rail systems, and NVIDIA's co-packaged optics — that is, the physical layer carrying every bit between and inside AI clusters. Demand is highly durable as long as AI capital spending and bandwidth-per-GPU keep scaling, with the indium-phosphide supply constraint giving it pricing power, though that durability is fully levered to the single AI-capex factor.
consensus_delta: The market is not looking the wrong way on Coherent — coverage is heavy (21-plus analysts, a consensus 'Buy', and an average price target of about $384, essentially at the current price), and the AI-optical story is fully discovered after a +171% run over 12 months to near all-time highs. So where we disagree with the market is thin, and it cuts in an unusual direction. The bull's point that the market under-models is Coherent's pricing power and the 6-inch indium-phosphide margin lever (margin mostly ahead, not yet banked), plus the breadth of stacked growth vectors (multi-rail and thermal are barely in the models). The bear's point is that the market is capitalizing the co-packaged-optics/optical-circuit-switching/multi-rail/thermal optionality at about 52x forward earnings and about 9–10x enterprise value to sales before it earns a dollar, while free cash flow is negative (−$383M in the March quarter) and return on invested capital is about 4%. The market assumption most worth flagging: that the NVIDIA partnership and co-packaged-optics market are incremental upside still to be priced in — they have already fired into the move. From here, the consensus and the actual results are roughly aligned, so there is no fat expectations gap to exploit. This is a momentum/quality name where the price already reflects the good news, not a contrarian re-rate.
valley_of_death: The near-term (3–9 month) risks are about valuation and crowded positioning, not solvency — the balance sheet is healthy (0.5x leverage, $3B of cash including NVIDIA's $2B, and no wall of maturing debt). (1) The free-cash-flow hole: capital spending is $290M and guided to rise from one quarter to the next while free cash flow runs negative — a high-beta (beta about 2.05), near-all-time-high stock that burns cash is acutely vulnerable to any multiple compression. (2) The FQ4 FY26 print (around early August 2026) is the binary event: it has to confirm accelerating growth and gross margin toward 42% — any margin stall (the conceded 'fall-through' question) or guide-down de-rates a 52x stock hard. (3) An AI-capex scare: per the regime note (CATALYST_WATCH_REGIME §5), AI capital spending is about 40% of 2026 S&P EPS growth and the single dominant factor under the market; a hyperscaler capex guide-down would sell off the entire optical cohort (Lumentum/Credo/Fabrinet/Coherent) together — Coherent has zero independence from this. (4) An indium-phosphide ramp slip would hit growth and margin at the same time. (5) A cohort/factor unwind: the Bank of America Fund Manager Survey shows cash at a 3.9% sell-signal level, and with the Magnificent Seven about 30% of the index there is little dry powder to absorb an AI-capex shock. The broader market (a complacent melt-up over a live oil/inflation tail, with the Fed on hold) raises the stakes on a name priced for perfection.
positioning_washout: There is no dividend-cut or yield-floor washout mechanism here (the dividend is tiny and the shareholder base is growth-oriented). The relevant forced-selling dynamic is the opposite of a value washout: Coherent is a momentum, Magnificent-Seven-adjacent AI-optical name with high beta (about 2.05), near all-time highs, up 171% in 12 months — it is owned by momentum, growth and passive/AI-thematic money, not by contrarian value investors. So the forced-selling risk is a de-grossing/momentum-reversal washout: on any AI-capex scare or factor rotation, the same crowded flows that drove the +171% reverse automatically and the whole optical cohort sells together (no independence). There is no clean capitulation flush to buy into here as the entry — if anything, the disciplined posture is to wait for exactly such a cohort de-gross to reset the entry, since right now you are buying near the high into crowded positioning.
forcing_function: Hard dates: (1) FQ4 FY26 earnings, around early August 2026 — the make-or-break print that confirms or breaks the accelerating-growth and margin-toward-42% trajectory, and the first read on optical-circuit-switching and co-packaged-optics revenue actually starting. (2) The second half of calendar 2026 — initial scale-out co-packaged-optics revenue has to begin (the NVIDIA-anchored milestone); a slip is a dent in the thesis. (3) The end of calendar 2026 — the milestone of doubling internal indium-phosphide capacity (guided a quarter early). (4) The first half of calendar 2027 — first multi-rail revenue; and the second half of calendar 2027 — first scale-up co-packaged-optics and thermal revenue. The most consequential near-term catalyst is the August print: that is where the market re-tests whether the +171% move is justified by accelerating fundamentals or has gotten ahead of them.
moderator_conclusion: Coherent is one of the highest-quality franchises in the scale-out optical basket — genuinely transformed (net leverage of 0.5x, down from 2.1x), margin-expanding (7 of 8 quarters, up about 5.3 percentage points / 530 basis points), demand-saturated (record backlog, orders into calendar 2028, the NVIDIA $2B anchor), under operator-style management that under-promises and over-delivers, with the broadest vertically-integrated photonics platform in the AI-interconnect bottleneck and real stacked optionality (optical circuit switching, co-packaged optics, multi-rail, thermal). The deep-dive's strengthening, credibility-4 read is fully earned. But applying the regime (CATALYST_WATCH §3/§5): the load-bearing catalyst — the NVIDIA co-packaged-optics partnership — has already fired into a +171% move over 12 months to near all-time highs, so this is no longer an asymmetric special situation; it is a momentum/quality name where the optionality is largely priced in. At about 52x forward earnings and about 9–10x enterprise value to sales with negative free cash flow and return on invested capital of about 4%, the sum-of-the-parts base (about $345) sits about 11% below the current price (about $389), with the bull at about $485 (+25%) versus the bear at about $250 (−36%) — a risk/reward of about 0.7 to 1 against you. Critically, §4/§5 flag that AI capital spending is the single dominant factor under the market (about 40% of 2026 EPS growth), and Coherent adds zero independent way for the bet to resolve to a basket already concentrated on that exact wall — a hyperscaler capex scare sells off the whole cohort together. The discipline call is to wait for the wash-out: own the franchise, but wait for a cohort-wide AI-capex de-gross / momentum-reversal flush (or a margin-disappointing print) to reset the entry below about $300, rather than paying near the all-time high for priced-in, single-factor optionality. Conviction 3. On the pure-value case (stripping out the catalyst overlay) it is also a 3: the sum-of-the-parts fair value is roughly at the current price (no margin of safety), and while the moat is widening and the secular threat manageable (so this is not a value-trap), the rich multiple on negative free cash flow plus weak current returns caps the standalone value enthusiasm at neutral. Great company, demanding price, wrong moment to chase.
interrogator_dossier:
# Coherent Corp. (COHR) — Forensic Deep Dive on Management and the Setup

*Scale-out Basket A (optical). Source: Coherent FQ3 FY2026 earnings call (May 6, 2026), plus FMP fundamentals and live web verification, June 2026. The fiscal year ends around June; FQ3 FY26 is the quarter ended March 31, 2026.*

## 1. Narrative arc
A clean two-act transformation under new operator-style management (CEO Jim Anderson, former Lattice CEO; CFO Sherri Luther). Act 1 (fiscal 2025): deleveraging, pruning the portfolio (sold Aerospace & Defense in FQ1 FY26, sold the Munich product division in FQ3 at below-corporate gross margin), repairing margins, and setting an above-42% adjusted gross-margin Investor-Day target. Act 2 (fiscal 2026, current): a decisive pivot to AI data-center optical infrastructure as a multi-year secular growth engine — Datacenter & Communications is now 75% of revenue, up about 40% year over year. Stacked growth vectors are introduced and layered in: 800G (shipping), 1.6T (ramping faster than expected), optical circuit switching (a market above $4B, doubled at the OFC conference, with the bottleneck cleared), co-packaged optics (a market above $15B, the NVIDIA partnership being $2B of equity plus a supply agreement through the end of the decade, scale-out in the second half of calendar 2026, scale-up in the second half of calendar 2027), multi-rail data-center interconnect (a market above $2B, first half of calendar 2027), and thermal/Thermadite (second half of calendar 2027). Industrial is soft but retained and re-cast as AI-thermal optionality. The master enabler woven through every answer is the 6-inch indium-phosphide capacity ramp (Sherman, Texas; Sweden; Zurich) — at once the volume unlock and the gross-margin lever.

## 2. Claims versus financials
Revenue of $1.806B, up about 21% reported and about 27% on a comparable basis year over year, up about 9% from the prior quarter — the acceleration is true and honestly adjusted for divestitures. Adjusted gross margin of 39.6%, up about 0.57 percentage points (57 basis points) from the prior quarter and about 1.05 percentage points (105 basis points) year over year (7 of 8 quarters up, a cumulative gain of about 5.3 percentage points / 530 basis points) — true, but still about 2.5 percentage points (250 basis points) short of the above-42% target. Adjusted EPS of $1.41, up about 55% year over year, with operating margin of 20.3% — the operating leverage is real; reported (GAAP) EPS was $0.97 (the gap is intangible amortization plus stock-based compensation, normal for a roll-up). Deleveraging to 0.5x net (from 2.1x a year ago), with $162M of debt paid down — the most impressive forensic data point; the balance sheet is transformed. Record backlog, orders into calendar 2028, and long-term agreements to the end of the decade — plausible but unquantified (no backlog figure disclosed); taken on management's word plus the NVIDIA anchor. The narrative is richer than the print: co-packaged optics, optical circuit switching, multi-rail and thermal contribute roughly zero revenue today, yet the market pays about 52x forward earnings and about 9–10x enterprise value to sales for future revenue. The print is richer than appreciated where it matters: the deleveraging is underrated; free cash flow is deeply negative (March-quarter −$383M, with capital spending of $290M and rising), but it is growth spending funded by NVIDIA cash, not distress.

## 3. Tone and confidence: rising, and specific. 'Demand remains exceptionally strong with no signs of attenuation… record backlog.' The supply-constrained-not-demand-constrained framing is the strongest up-cycle tell. The posture is offensive: research and development raised to 9.9% of revenue, sales and administrative costs cut to 9.4%. There are dated milestone commitments (indium-phosphide doubling by the end of calendar 2026, a quarter early; co-packaged optics in the second half of 2026 and second half of 2027; multi-rail in the first half of 2027; thermal in the second half of 2027). The only defensive note is Industrial softness, spun forward into thermal. There is no hedging on macro or AI capital spending — which is itself the risk.

## 4. Guidance credibility: high. The CFO's '7 of 8 quarters of gross-margin expansion, up 530 basis points' is delivered and falsifiable. The Q4 guide ($1.91–2.05B in revenue, 39–41% gross margin, $1.52–1.72 EPS) implies accelerating quarter-over-quarter growth, consistent with the legible 2-to-3-month lag from indium-phosphide to transceiver. Under-promising on the indium-phosphide doubling (now a quarter early) is mild sandbagging and over-delivery. Open question: the roughly 250-basis-point climb to above-42% gross margin is still owed. There has been no pattern of misses since the leadership change.

## 5. Analyst pressure points: (a) Gross-margin fall-through (Barclays/Jefferies) — 'why not more incremental margin given 6-inch indium phosphide at half the cost?' — the answer concedes that the margin upside is mostly ahead and execution-dependent. (b) The competitive gap versus Lumentum on optical circuit switching and co-packaged optics (Raymond James) — deflected into market-size rhetoric without quantifying Coherent's own optical-circuit-switching/co-packaged-optics revenue, a real non-answer. (c) The capacity-to-revenue lag (TD Cowen) — answered honestly (a 2-to-3-month device-to-transceiver lag). (d) The scale of 1.6T — declined to break out revenue by data rate. (e) Silicon-photonics viability at 3.2T (TD Cowen) — rebutted with an OFC 400G-per-lane demo; the roadmap debate is live.

## 6. Red and green flags. Green: genuine deleveraging (0.5x from 2.1x); the NVIDIA $2B plus supply agreement; supply-constrained with record backlog plus long-term agreements that include customer upfront capital; margin expansion delivered with the 6-inch indium-phosphide cost lever mostly ahead; new management over-delivering; stacked, real optionality. Red: deeply negative free cash flow (−$383M in the March quarter, with capital spending rising); valuation is the whole bear case (about $76B market value, about 52x forward earnings, about 9–10x enterprise value to sales, up 171% over 12 months near all-time highs); the above-42% gross-margin target is unmet (39.6%); a single shared macro driver (AI capital spending) means zero independence within the basket; Industrial is shrinking; and the competitive share in optical circuit switching and co-packaged optics is unquantified.

## 7. Hidden signals: pricing power is a quiet gross-margin tailwind ('pricing optimization' recurs, with a price step-up at each data-rate transition); vertical integration is a moat ('we provide all the ingredients, not just the laser' — indium phosphide, fiber, lens arrays, isolators, and thermoelectric coolers); market-size inflation is a tell (optical circuit switching doubled, co-packaged optics called 'conservative' above $15B in a single quarter — bullish, but also how a rich stock keeps the narrative ahead of the print); 'allocate indium-phosphide capacity to maximum margin dollars' is disciplined, Lattice-style capital allocation; and the 6-inch indium-phosphide ramp is the master variable — if it slips, growth and margin wobble together.

## 8. Capital-allocation verdict: strengthening. Genuinely transformed — deleveraged, margin-expanding, demand-saturated, NVIDIA-anchored, with operator-style management hitting dated milestones. The single thing the director must weigh: how much AI-optical optionality is already in the price (not cheap: about 52x forward earnings, up 171% over a year, near highs; co-packaged optics, optical circuit switching, multi-rail and thermal are future revenue; it burns free cash flow today). Moat: narrow but real, trending wider — intangible (photonics intellectual property plus the 6-inch indium-phosphide process) plus scale-cost (vertically integrated bill of materials) plus emerging switching costs (long-term agreements, the NVIDIA co-packaged-optics design-in); returns are depressed today (return on invested capital about 4%, return on equity about 5%) by growth spending and the intangible base, but margins are rising, not falling, and the pricing power is real, so the moat is widening, not eroding. Secular threat: manageable — arguably a beneficiary; it sits on the winning side of AI scale-out, and the terminal risk is the cyclicality of AI capital spending (demand durability, not moat erosion) plus technology-transition risk (copper hanging on, rival co-packaged optics, the silicon-photonics-versus-electro-absorption-modulated-laser fork), both manageable given the breadth of the laser roadmap and the NVIDIA design-in. Not cheap, so neither a fear-print nor a value-trap — a high-quality growth name where the debate is entirely about how much of the future is already paid for.

Management credibility 4 of 5; trajectory strengthening; narrow but real moat; moat trend widening; secular threat manageable.
market_cap: $76B (~$76.2B on ~195.6M shares at ~$389; note FMP's $61.8B uses a stale ~155.5M share count pre-NVDA/equity issuance)
skeptic_kill_fact: There is no single fact that breaks the case. The adversarial attack failed to break the load-bearing facts: every figure verifies against primary or near-primary sources and is current (after the NVIDIA deal and the FQ3 print of May 6, 2026), so there is no stale anchor. The NVIDIA $2B strategic investment plus a multi-year, multi-billion-dollar co-packaged-optics purchase commitment is real and confirmed by primary sources (NVIDIA investor relations and Coherent's 8-K filing, March 2, 2026). FQ3 FY26 revenue of about $1.8B (up about 27% on a comparable basis year over year) and the Q4 guide ($1.91–2.05B in revenue, $1.52–1.72 EPS) verify; net leverage of 0.5x (from 2.1x), cash of about $3B including NVIDIA's $2B, capital spending of $290M (rising), and a share count of 195.64M all confirm. Because this is a scale-out skeptic on a name whose director verdict is already to wait for the wash-out and whose thesis is itself the bear case (a price problem, not a company problem), the verified facts reinforce the bear: the stock at about $389 (versus a 52-week high of $440 and an all-time-high close of $426.89 on 2026-06-02), about 52x forward earnings, about 11.7x enterprise value to sales (richer than the dossier's stated 9–10x), with trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of roughly −$538M and the NVIDIA/co-packaged-optics catalyst already fired into the +171% move, is priced for perfection with zero basket independence from the single AI-capex factor. The bear case survives the kill-check.
skeptic_corrections: (1) The valuation was understated in the bull's favor: enterprise value to sales is about 11.7x (stockanalysis.com, June 2026), not the dossier's about 9–10x — the name is richer than stated, which strengthens the bear, not weakens it. (2) The free-cash-flow figure: the bear cites March-quarter free cash flow of −$383M; the verifiable consolidated number is trailing-twelve-month free cash flow of about −$538M on trailing-twelve-month operating cash flow of about +$140M — directionally identical (deeply negative, capital-spending-driven) and if anything worse; the single-quarter −$383M is plausible but not separately confirmed by primary sources, so cite the trailing-twelve-month figure. (3) 'Net debt $1.83B' is stale relative to the post-NVIDIA balance sheet: total debt of about $3.19B less about $3B of cash is roughly net-neutral, not $1.83B of net debt — immaterial to the per-share math, but the sum-of-the-parts text should not lean on a $1.83B net-debt figure.
skeptic_evidence: NVIDIA Investor Relations / press release, 2026-03-02: 'NVIDIA and Coherent Announce Strategic Partnership to Develop Optics Technology' — $2B NVIDIA investment in Coherent + non-exclusive multi-billion-dollar NVIDIA purchase commitment + future access/capacity rights, five additional CPO-tied product families (investor.nvidia.com).
Coherent Corp. Form 8-K (SEC EDGAR CIK 0000820318, accession 000119312526084366, ex-99.1), filed March 2026 — primary filing of the NVIDIA strategic investment terms.

Coherent FQ3-FY26 results (quarter ended 2026-03-31, reported 2026-05-06): record revenue ~$1.8B (+27% pro-forma YoY, +9% QoQ), Q4 guide $1.91-2.05B / EPS $1.52-1.72, net leverage 0.5x (from 2.1x), capex $290M, cash ~$3B incl. NVDA $2B — Coherent earnings call / Motley Fool & Globe and Mail transcripts, 2026-05-06.

stockanalysis.com/stocks/cohr (data as of 2026-06-18): price $389.57, market cap $76.22B, shares 195.64M, fwd P/E 52.33, EV/Sales 11.70, TTM OCF +$140.34M, TTM FCF -$538.24M; macrotrends: 52wk high $440, all-time-high close $426.89 on 2026-06-02.

scale.role: Broadest vertically-integrated photonics platform and the InP supply node for 800G/1.6T transceivers + the NVDA CPO partner ($2B equity, supply through end-of-decade); the toll every optical-interconnect dollar pays.
scale.rationale: Purest least-redundant optical CORE alongside FN: supply-constrained (orders into CY2028, LTAs to end of decade), the 6-inch InP ramp is both the volume unlock and the GM lever. This is the indispensable franchise I would actually seat in the layer — an 800-level CORE on importance + demand-durability + competitive position. Skeptic verdict CONFIRMED_WITH_CORRECTIONS, but the only correction is that the name is RICHER than the dossier stated (EV/sales ~11.7x not 9-10x; net-debt framing) — a valuation footnote, not a franchise impairment — so a small ~8pt haircut off an ~88 franchise. Catalyst FIRED (+171% TTM) so no near-term asymmetry; the richness lives in valuation_posture, not the conviction number.
scale.demand_durability: Very high — DataCom 75% of revenue +40% YoY, bolted directly to hyperscaler optical capex via multi-year LTAs.
